content_preview.title: 63-Year Ocean Dataset Closes the Sea Level Budget Gap, Confirming Oceans Rose Nearly 10 Centimeters Since 1960 at a Doubling Pace
content_preview.summary: A Science Advances study reconciles six decades of sea level data, resolving a long-standing measurement discrepancy and pinpointing ocean warming and ice melt as drivers of an accelerating rise.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A new study published in [*Science Advances*](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aea0652) has resolved a decades-long gap between observed sea level measurements and what scientists could account for from known physical causes. Drawing on 63 years of ocean observations from 1960 to 2023, the research finds that global mean sea level has risen at 2.06 millimeters per year over the full period — and that the pace has nearly doubled, reaching 3.94 millimeters per year between 2005 and 2023...

recommendations: Consider adding doi.org and trusted science news aggregators to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured climate science news article. Clear organization with Overview, What We Know, Closing the Budget Gap, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Word count 716 fits the News category. The research is genuine and significant.
editor_notes.source_verification: 5 of 6 sources verified substantively. source-0.html.gz (ScienceDaily): confirmed contribution percentages (43% thermal, 27% glaciers, 15% Greenland, 12% Antarctica, 3% land water), 2.06 and 3.94 mm/yr rates. source-1.html.gz (EurekaAlert): returned bot-blocking page (202 with JS required) — not usable but article doesn't cite EurekaAlert inline. source-2.html.gz (SciTechDaily): confirmed John Abraham quote verbatim and author list. source-3.html.gz (Eos): confirmed 41% ocean warming and 21% land water storage for rate acceleration, confirmed policymaker statement. source-4.html.gz (Above the Norm News): confirmed 0.71 mm/yr per decade acceleration, 0.17 mm/yr residual, Greenland 0.29 to 0.70 mm/yr, Antarctica 0.23 to 0.51 mm/yr, 1 billion people figure and country list. source-5.html.gz (DOI/Science Advances via Archive.org): only redirect page; paper title and DOI match claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key quantitative claims verified: rates (2.06, 3.94, 3.41 mm/yr), contribution percentages, ice sheet trajectories, residual gap (0.17 mm/yr from 0.33 mm/yr IPCC AR6), acceleration parameter (0.71 mm/yr/decade), and John Abraham quote. No fabrications detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The analysis section appropriately contextualizes the stakes without sensationalizing.
editor_notes.originality: No prior sea level rise articles in the 2026-05 archive. The study's publication date (May 2026) and content are timely.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science journalism article. All verifiable facts trace correctly to cited sources. APPROVE.
